title: The Constitutional Convention and debates over ratification
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on the Constitutional Convention and the debates over ratification
topic_path: The Constitutional Convention and debates over ratification | 3.8
The Constitutional Convention and debates over ratification

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8f0eeca3336cc9a0da16fee2cc30a91a.png)

A political cartoon published in The Massachusetts Centinel on August 2nd, 1788. 
Image credit: [Library of Congress](http://www.loc.gov/pictures/resource/cph.3a45782/)

**Which of the following events was this image responding to? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: states ratifying the Constitution
colonial participation in the Revolutionary War
colonies ratifying the Articles of Confederation
states ratifying the Bill of Rights

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8f0eeca3336cc9a0da16fee2cc30a91a.png)

A political cartoon published in The Massachusetts Centinel on August 2nd, 1788. 
Image credit: [Library of Congress](http://www.loc.gov/pictures/resource/cph.3a45782/)

**Federalists were able to reach a compromise with Anti-Federalists over the strength of the new central government by agreeing to which of the following? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a system of checks and balances within the federal government
a separation of powers within each state government
an end to the Atlantic slave trade after 1808
a two-term limit for all members of Congress

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **ESTIMATES OF ENSLAVED PEOPLE OF AFRICAN DESCENT SENT TO MAINLAND NORTH AMERICA VIA THE ATLANTIC SLAVE TRADE, 1751-1840** ||
Years | Number of enslaved people 
:-: | :-: 
$1751-1760$ | $42,947$
$1761-1770$ | $46,691$
$1771-1780$ | $31,185$
$1781-1790$ | $14,298$
$1791-1800$ | $14,338$ 
$1801-1810$ | $72,978$
$1811-1820$ | $4,635$
$1821-1830$ | $91$ 
$1831-1840$ | $0$
>Adapted from Herbert S. Klein, The Atlantic Slave Trade, 2010


**Which of the following best explains the changes in the population of enslaved Africans from 1801 to 1820?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: America ended the Atlantic slave trade in 1808 as a condition of the Three-Fifths Compromise.
The Three-Fifths Compromise ended slavery in three-fifths of the colonies, reducing the amount of enslaved Africans.
The Three-Fifths Compromise ended slavery in the United States in 1808, reducing the amount of enslaved Africans.
America reduced the number of enslaved Africans brought through the slave trade by two-fifths as part of the Three-Fifths Compromise.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In being compelled to labor, not for himself, but for a master. . . the slave may appear to be degraded from the human rank, and classed with those irrational animals which fall under the legal denomination of property. In being protected, on the other hand, in his life and in his limbs, against the violence of all others, even the master of his labor and his liberty; and in being punishable himself for all violence committed against others, the slave is no less evidently regarded by the law as a member of the society, not as a part of the irrational creation; as a moral person, not as a mere article of property. The federal Constitution, therefore, decides with great propriety on the case of our slaves, when it views them in the mixed character of persons and of property. This is in fact their true character.”

>-Source: *The Federalist Papers*, No. 54, 1788


**Which of the following Constitutional compromises does this excerpt support?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Three-Fifths Compromise
The Great Compromise
The creation of the Electoral College
The inclusion of a Bill of Rights

**Which of the following events best represents a continuity of the sentiments expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Supreme Court decision in *Dred Scott v. Sandford* in 1857
regional differences over the expansion of slavery west in the 1820s
support for the expansion of suffrage to minority groups in the 1840s
the Back-to-Africa movement led by Marcus Garvey in the 1920s

In being protected, on the other hand, in his life and in his limbs, against the violence of all others, even the master of his labor and his liberty; and in being punishable himself for all violence committed against others, the slave is no less evidently regarded by the law as a member of the society, not as a part of the irrational creation; as a moral person, not as a mere article of property. This is in fact their true character.”

**Which of the following best describes the core ideas behind the Three-Fifths Compromise?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enslaved persons were counted as three-fifths of a person for purposes of representation.
For every five states added to the United States, three had to be slave states.
Enslaved persons had to be paid for at least three years of service after they’ve been enslaved for five years.
For reelection, a member of the House of Representatives could only serve three consecutive terms every five years.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“It may be a reflection on human nature, that such devices should be necessary to control the abuses of government. But what is government itself, but the greatest of all reflections on human nature? If men were angels, no government would be necessary. If angels were to govern men, neither external nor internal controls on government would be necessary. In framing a government which is to be administered by men over men, the great difficulty lies in this: you must first enable the government to control the governed; and in the next place oblige it to control itself.”

>-Source: *The Federalist Papers*, No. 51, 1788

**The ideas about government expressed in the excerpt are most consistent with which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the concept of checks and balances
the belief in universal suffrage
the principle of freedom of speech
the ideas of the First Great Awakening

assessment_items.question_content:

But what is government itself, but the greatest of all reflections on human nature? If men were angels, no government would be necessary. If angels were to govern men, neither external nor internal controls on government would be necessary. In framing a government which is to be administered by men over men, the great difficulty lies in this: you must first enable the government to control the governed; and in the next place oblige it to control itself.”

**Which of the Founders would be most likely to disagree with Madison’s statements in the excerpt above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Patrick Henry
John Jay
George Washington
Alexander Hamilton

assessment_items.question_content:
>“It may be a reflection on human nature, that such devices should be necessary to control the abuses of government. But what is government itself, but the greatest of all reflections on human nature? If men were angels, no government would be necessary. If angels were to govern men, neither external nor internal controls on government would be necessary. In framing a government which is to be administered by men over men, the great difficulty lies in this: you must first enable the government to control the governed; and in the next place oblige it to control itself.”

>-Source: *The Federalist Papers*, No. 51, 1788

**How were the Federalists and Anti-Federalists able to reach a compromise to ratify the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Federalists guaranteed that they would vote to add a Bill of Rights to the Constitution as a series of Amendments.
The Federalists guaranteed that enslaved Africans would be counted as three-fifths of a person for representation purposes.
The Federalists guaranteed that the President would have a two-term limit and not a lifetime appointment.
The Federalists guaranteed that the federal government would  not have the power to tax the states.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“In the Bill of Rights for this Commonwealth it is declared that the happiness of the people & the Preservation of civil government depend upon the piety religion & morality & that the people have a right to invest their Legislature with power to require that provision be made for the public worship of God & the support of [P]rotestant teachers & require the attendance of people upon such worship instructions.... We must insist that the Continental Constitution contain a Bill of Rights which by Express shall secure to us our privileges especially our religion.”

>-Source: Daniel Adams, at a town meeting in Townshend, Massachusetts, 1787

**The ideas in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the ratification of the first ten amendments to protect individual liberties and rights
the creation of the Electoral College as a way to elect the president
the development of a bicameral legislature, including a House of Representatives and a Senate
the compromise to include a passage in Article II that guaranteed the separation of church and state

assessment_items.question_content:

We must insist that the Continental Constitution contain a Bill of Rights which by Express shall secure to us our privileges especially our religion.”

>-Source: Daniel Adams, at a town meeting in Townshend, Massachusetts, 1787

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitutional Convention
The Seven Years’ War
The Great Awakening
The XYZ Affair

We must insist that the Continental Constitution contain a Bill of Rights which by Express shall secure to us our privileges especially our religion.”

**Based on the excerpt, the author was most likely a member of which group?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Anti-Federalists
The Federalists
The Old Lights
The New Lights
